coredata.dc:title: Promoting potential direct interspecies electron transfer (DIET) and methanogenesis with nitrogen and zinc doped carbon quantum dots

coredata.dc:description: Although it has been demonstrated that one-dimensional, two-dimensional, and three-dimensional carbon nanomaterials can improve the CH4 production of anaerobic digestion (AD), the effect of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials on AD have not been reported. To expand the application of carbon nanomaterials in AD, the effect of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials-carbon quantum dots (CDs) on various feedstocks (c.a. cellulose, glucose, ethanol, and vinegar residue) were investigated in this study. Results have shown that CH4 yield from ethanol was increased by 24.59% (p = 0.396) after adding 5 g/L zinc doped carbon quantum dots (Zn-doped CDs) while that from vinegar residue was dramatically increased by 230% (p = 0.000) using 5 g/L nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-doped CDs). In addition, photoluminescence demonstrated that CDs acted as a capacitor for transmitting and receiving electrons. Furthermore, co-occurrence network analysis revealed that Clostridiales might be used as a signal source to communicate with other species. This study firstly shifted the application of CDs from fluorescence to AD and manifested its positive impact on AD. In short, these findings provided a better understanding on the effects of CDs on different feedstocks of methanogenesis and revealed new evidence of stimulating methanogenesis via CDs.

head.ce:title: Promoting potential direct interspecies electron transfer (DIET) and methanogenesis with nitrogen and zinc doped carbon quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although it has been demonstrated that one-dimensional, two-dimensional, and three-dimensional carbon nanomaterials can improve the CH4 production of anaerobic digestion (AD), the effect of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials on AD have not been reported. To expand the application of carbon nanomaterials in AD, the effect of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials-carbon quantum dots (CDs) on various feedstocks (c.a. cellulose, glucose, ethanol, and vinegar residue) were investigated in this study. Results have shown that CH4 yield from ethanol was increased by 24.59% (p = 0.396) after adding 5 g/L zinc doped carbon quantum dots (Zn-doped CDs) while that from vinegar residue was dramatically increased by 230% (p = 0.000) using 5 g/L nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-doped CDs). In addition, photoluminescence demonstrated that CDs acted as a capacitor for transmitting and receiving electrons. Furthermore, co-occurrence network analysis revealed that Clostridiales might be used as a signal source to communicate with other species. This study firstly shifted the application of CDs from fluorescence to AD and manifested its positive impact on AD. In short, these findings provided a better understanding on the effects of CDs on different feedstocks of methanogenesis and revealed new evidence of stimulating methanogenesis via CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, AD of organic biomass to generate methane provides a sustainable approach for reducing greenhouse gas emissions and decreasing consumption of fossil fuel (Li et al., 2019). However, the efficiency and stability of AD are susceptible to various biological and environmental factors, such as process temperature (Li et al., 2014), pH (Li et al., 2020), fluid dynamics (Leonzio, 2019), organic loading rate (Bi et al., 2019), and residence time (Kamyab et al., 2019). Electron transfer plays a crucial role in the methanogenesis process. Bacteria transports electrons to extracellular electron acceptors for respiration and sharing of energynutrients with syntrophic partners (Wang et al., 2019). Interspecies electron transfer is an electronic flow pattern between syntrophic microbes including interspecies hydrogen transfer, inter-formic acid transfer, and direct interspecies electron transfer (DIET). Cruz Viggi et al. (2014) demonstrated that DIET is more effective between exoelectrogenic cells compared to interspecies electron transfer, such as hydrogen and formic acid electron transfer. Therefore, DIET acceleration is likely to be a feasible process to increase methane production (Lei et al., 2016).

DIET can be linked by bioelectricity and considered as combination of biotic and abiotic electrons. DIET has been reported in co-culture systems of Geobacter species based on biological electrical connections (Zhao et al., 2015). Various conductive materials, such as granular activated carbon (GAC) (Liu et al., 2012), biochar (Luo et al., 2015), carbon cloth (Zhao et al., 2017), carbon nanotube (Li et al., 2015b) were reported in associate with improving DIET and enhancing AD performance. Addition of GAC could enhance the activity of Methanosarcina when AD of ethanol, as it provided a surface for cells to attach to promote DIET between microorganisms and improved the methanogenic performance (Zhao et al., 2016). Lee et al. found that production rate of methane increased by 1.8 times after adding GAC in the continuous flow reactor. This is due to the enrichment of Geobacter, Thauera, and Gordonia by GAC. Especially for the enrichment of Geobacter, GAC is usually used as an electron acceptor, thereby improving the performance of methane production (Lee et al., 2016). Carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon, carbon cloth, and graphene, are generally believed good for electron transfer in the process of methane production, for instance, addition of 1.0 g/L graphene can increase methane production by over 20%. The reason is that the addition of graphene in AD can replace conductive fimbriae, and can help Pseudomonas transfer electrons to methanogens during AD process, thereby accelerating the growth of Pseudomonas. In addition, the enrichment of Geobacteria and Pseudomonas accelerates DIET in AD of ethanol (Lin et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2015). Furthermore, its application has extra benefits on stabilizing AD process under inhibitory condition. Lu et al. demonstrated that addition of biochar, carbon, carbon nanotubes, GAC, or nano-graphene could enhance the stability of AD process at ammonia concentration above 7000 mg/L (Lu et al., 2016).

As a carbon-based nanomaterial, CDs has similar characteristics to carbon nanotubes, graphene, or fullerene with excellent electrical, physical, and optical properties (Hola et al., 2014; Jariwala et al., 2013). Recently, CDs has become one of the most promising nanomaterials which has intensively studied as it has various advantages, such as low toxicity and “green” preparation, easy to realize surface functionalization, stable chemical properties, excellent catalytic performance. However, its application are limited on preparing fluorescent materials (Resch-Genger et al., 2008), biological imaging (Liu et al., 2015), and medicine (Ge et al., 2014), while the impact on AD has not been fully explored. Application of CDs is not generally accepted that the electron transfer during the methanogenesis process is dominated by DIET. Based on these considerations, this topic selects carbon-based materials that researchers rarely try CDs, a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial to study the influence of CDs on AD system and analyze the mechanism.

Thus, the objectives of this study were to investigate the effect of exogenous added materials on AD and gain deep understanding of the mechanism of CDs as a nanomaterial to promote potential DIET. CDs were synthesized using hydrothermal methods in Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Analytical methods, including SEM, photoluminescence spectra, XPS, FTIR, and microbial community analysis were employed to determine the physical and chemical properties of CDs. Both kinetics simulation and electron flux calculation were conducted. Furthermore, analysis of co-occurrence network and microbial community structures were carried out to have insight on the impact of CDs addition in AD process.

N-doped CDs were synthesized using the following protocol; 25 mL sodium citrate solution (0.1 M) and 300 μL ethane diamine (ρ = 0.9 g/cm3) were added into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, which was further kept at 180 ℃ for 6 h. Afterwards, the mixtures were filtered using filtration membrane filter (0.22 µm) to obtain N-doped CDs. Preparation of Zn-doped CDs followed the similar protocol; mixture of 25 mL of sodium citrate (0.1 M) and zinc chloride (0.05 M) were added into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, which was maintained at 185 ℃ for 4 h. Subsequently, the product was filtered with a cylindrical filtration membrane filter (0.22 µm) to obtain the final Zn-doped CDs.

This study carried out four batches experiments. Inoculum used in this study was collected from the Asuwei Landfill (Beijing, China). Prior to start, 0.5 g/L glucose was added and acclimated at 37 ℃ for 1 month. A batch of inoculum will be domesticated before each batch of tests to prepare for the test. Therefore, the inoculum properties of each batch test are not the same, and the properties need to be determined before the test, including total solid (TS) and volatile solid (VS).
In this study, the influence of CDs on four feedstocks has been investigated. The experimental design parameters are shown in Table 1.

There were in total four feedstocks used in the study, that can be categorized as (1) raw material (glucose, cellulose, and ethanol); (2) complicated processing residues (vinegar residue). Glucose, ethanol, and cellulose were purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Factory (Beichen District, Tianjin, China) and Beijing Chemical Plant (Daxing District, Beijing, China), respectively. Vinegar residue were in the air for three days before conducting experiments supplied by industrial vinegar factory (Shanxi, China), which is the main by-product after solid-state fermentation (SSF) process.

First, take the substrate and the acclimated inoculum (working volume: 250 mL) into a 500 mL fermentation flask, using CDs (N-doped CDs, Zn-doped CDs) as additives. In addition, a blank group (containing only 250 mL of inoculation liquid) is set to deduct the gas production error of the inoculation liquid itself. All experiments were set to 37 °C moderate-temperature AD with a rotation speed of 90 rpm, and each experiment was performed in triplicate. The specific experimental parameters are shown in the
Tables 2 and 3.

Methane production was determined using an AMPTS-II system (Bioprocess Control, Sweden). The anaerobic fermentation bottles were placed in a water bath to keep constant temperature during the entire period. Biogas was purified by passing through the absorption bottle containing NaOH to remove CO2 and H2S. After purification, the generated CH4 was recorded by a gas meter. Specific experimental methods can be found in Supplementary Material.

After AD, 10 mL of liquid digestate and 5 g solid digestate were taken out from AD bottles. These samples were flashed with phosphate buffered saline, then centrifuged at 4 ℃ for 10 min and stored at −20 ℃. All these samples were sent to Beijing Microsin Technology Co., Ltd. for high-throughput sequencing to obtain the diversity of microbial communities. The process of sample sequencing was as follows: First, the microbial DNA is extracted from the sample, amplified by a PCR instrument, and the obtained PCR product is purified, and then the extracted product is quantified and homogenized, and finally Illumina sequencing. The V3-V4 hypervariable regions of the bacteria 16 S rRNA gene were amplified with primers 338 F (5’-ACTCCTACGGGAGGCAGC AG-3’) and 806R (5’-GGACTACHVGGGTWTCTAAT3’) by thermocycler PCR system (GeneAmp 9700, ABI, USA). specific sequencing methods according to (Guo et al., 2020). The data were analyzed on the free online platform of Majorbio I-Sanger Cloud Platform (http://www.i-sanger.com).

TS and VS are obtained by standard methods of the American Public Health Association (McCrady, 1966). Elemental compositions (C, H, and N) of all feedstocks were determined by an organic element analyzer (EA3000, Leeman China, Italy). Oxygen content was calculated by assuming C + H + O + N = 99.5% on a VS basis (Rincón et al., 2012). Energy efficiency calculation is based on references (Li et al., 2013). Fluorescence measurements were carried out with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FS5 from Techcomp (China) Ltd), and the quantum yield (QY) was measured using a FS-30 quantum yield accessory with an integrating sphere. Three mL distilled water were excited at 350 nm and the emission spectrum was recorded as control. Then, 15 μL the samples of purified CDs solution were diluted to 3 mL and measured under the same conditions. The absorption of samples was lower than 0.1 a.u. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded using ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with a mono X-Ray source Al Ka excitation (1486.6 eV). Binding energy calibration was based on C1s at 284.6 eV. The ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra of the samples were measured using a UV–Vis spectrometer (Jasco V-570). A Bruker Vertex 70 V spectrometer was used for the measurement of the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra. The morphology of the prepared CDs was investigated via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Model JEM-2100). The conductivity of the CDs was measured by dissolving the obtained CDs powder in water formulated into 1 g/L solution to measure the conductivity of the solution. One-way ANOVA analysis of variance was performed using SPSS (IBM, NY 10504, USA) and LSD method has been employed to evaluate the variance homogeneity ( Table 4).

In this study, modified Gompertz model and Cone model was used to evaluate AD system (Donoso-Bravo et al., 2010; Li et al., 2012, 2015a; Pagliaccia et al., 2016; Pitt et al., 1999; Wang et al., 2013), where B (mL/g VS) represents the cumulative methane production for a digestion time t; B0 (mL/g VS) is the ultimate methane production of the feedstock; k (day–1) is the first-order reduction rate constant; μ
m is the maximum methane production rate (mL/g VS/day); λ is the lag time (day); t is digestion time (day); e is the base of a natural logarithm (2.7183); n means the shape factor (dimensionless).

The XPS spectra of C1s, O1s, and Zn2p of the obtained Zn-doped CDs are presented in Fig. 1a–c, C1s and N1s of the obtained N-doped CDs are presented in Figs. 1d–e. In the C1s spectrum (Fig. 1a), two distinct carbon components appear at 288.2 eV and 284.8 eV, respectively, which could be attributed to C˭O and C–C bonding (Qu et al., 2014). The O1s spectra peak centered at 530.8 eV (Fig. 1b) can be assigned to C–O binding (Jiang et al., 2015). The Zn2p spectrum (Fig. 1c) presents two predominant features at 1043.5 eV and 1020.5 eV, corresponding to Zn2p1/3 and Zn2p2/3, respectively. In Fig. 1d, the C1s spectrum of N-doped CD, four distinct carbon components are found at 288.3 eV, 286.5 eV, 285.6 eV and 284.6 eV, which could be attributed to C˭O, C–O, C–N, and C–C bonding, respectively. In Fig. 1e, the N1s signal centered at 399.5 eV agrees to the N–H binding. The XPS spectrum of N1s strongly indicated the existing of N after ethylenediamine treatment ( Fig. 2).

FTIR spectroscopy were used to investigate the functional groups in the prepared N-doped and Zn-doped CDs. The FTIR spectrum of N-doped CDs and the XPS results demonstrated the presence of the same groups; the peak at 3300 cm–1 is attributed to N–H bonds (Silva et al., 2017). The peak at 2950 cm–1 can be assigned to the C–H group while the peak at 1595 cm–1 is due to the existence of C˭O stretching vibration. The peak at 1263 cm–1 is ascribed to C–O bonds while the peak at 848 cm–1 is attributed to ˭C–H bonds. Furthermore, the peak at 1396 cm–1 can be assigned to the C–N group. FTIR and XPS analyses provide further evidence for N incorporation into the CDs via C–N bonds, where N atoms are crucial to quantum confinement and edge effects improvement as they could provide more active sites and modify electronic features of CDs. For Zn-doped CDs, the presence of polar OH group on Zn-doped CDs surface is due to the excellent water solubility characteristic of CDs (Peng et al., 2012). The peak at 3515 cm–1 is attributed to O–H. In addition, there is a peak at 3250 cm–1 represent of the C−H bond. The two sharp peaks at 1655 and 1573 cm–1 represent the typical stretching vibrations of C˭O and C˭C, which can be electron acceptors and electron donors and these functional groups may contribute greatly to DIET. Study has shown that biochar is a complex material having unique properties such as redox properties (oxygen-containing functional group or double bond) besides conductivity, which makes it a good candidate for enhancing methanogenesis in the enriched culture systems (Yuan et al., 2018). Therefore, it is speculated that these oxygen-containing functional groups of CDs may play an important role in AD.

The TEM image ( Figs. 3b and d) shows that the CDs were well separated from each other. The average diameter of prepared N-doped CDs was 6.84 nm. Meanwhile, the average diameter of prepared Zn-doped CD was 6.08 nm. In addition, the particle sizes of the CDs are mostly distributed in the range of 5–9 nm (Fig. 3a). More than 70% of the N-doped CDs had dimensions between 6 and 8 nm and more than 80% of the Zn-doped CDs had dimensions between 5 and 8 nm indicating the synthetic process had high level of size selectivity.

The photoluminescence of the CDs was investigated using a fluorescence spectrometer. An intense emission centered at 440 nm (λem) was observed from N-doped CDs when excited at wavelengths ranged from 300 to 400 nm ( Fig. 4a), while Zn-doped CDs had the strongest photoluminescence intensity under the best excitation (340 nm) and emission (440 nm). The variation of excited wavelength from 300 to 400 nm for N-doped CDs and Zn-doped CDs resulted in an intensity decreasing of emission without peak location change, which indicates the excitation-dependent emission properties of the obtained CDs. N-doped CDs and Zn-doped CDs are both transparent solution under natural sunlight but emitted a strong blue fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp (inset of Fig. 4a and b). The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the N-doped CDs and Zn-doped CDs were shown in Fig. 3c. The N-doped CDs UV-Vis spectrum contains two domain peaks centered on 245 and 330 nm, while the Zn-doped CDs has only one prominent absorption around 240 nm, indicating that these samples possessed different surface states. Moreover, the photoluminescence lifetime of the derived Zn-doped CDs was 8.32 ns based on the observation (Fig. 4d), and the absolute quantum yield of CDs was quantified as 41.9% by calculating the peak area from fluorescence spectrometer. The photoluminescence property of CDs can be attributed to the surface defects, which could act as a capacitor for energy storage, thus promoting enzyme activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methane production performance after adding CDs during AD process
ce:section.ce:para: Cumulative and daily methane yield of cellulose are shown in Fig. 5a and b. For cellulose, CH4 production of the control (SMY-cellulose-control), 0.1 g/L N-doped CDs (SMY-cellulose-0.1 N-doped CDs), and 1.0 N-doped CDs (SMY-cellulose-1.0 N-doped CDs) was determined to be 162.2 ± 8.2, 146.2 ± 3.0, and 186.5 ± 14.5 mL/g VS, respectively. The maximum methane yield of cellulose with SMY-cellulose-1.0 N-doped CDs was a significant enhance (41.0 mL/g VS/d), which was 60.26% higher than SMY-cellulose-control (p = 0.136). However, the cumulative CH4 production of SMY-cellulose-0.1 N-doped CDs was firstly higher during the initial 19 days while, at the end, turned to be 10.9% lower than SMY-cellulose-control. Moreover, Lin et al. proposed that in the application of nanomaterials in AD, cytotoxicity has become a limiting factor. However, the toxicological molecular mechanism of nanomaterials is still limited, but the possible explanation is related to the synergistic effect of cell membrane perturbation and oxidative stress (Lin et al., 2017). The possible explanation could be that the concentration of N-doped CDs was not enough to have a positive effect on AD. Consistently, compared with SMY-cellulose-control, the methanogenic efficiency of SMY-cellulose-1.0 N-doped CDs increased by 14.98%, while that of SMY-cellulose-0.1 N-doped CDs decreased by 9.84% (Table S3). Therefore, the concentration of N-doped CDs is critical to the effects of CDs in AD.

Addition of CDs also showed a significant impact on the methane production using glucose. Cumulative CH4 production of the control (SMY-glucose-control), 0.1 g/L N-doped CDs (SMY-glucose-0.1 N-doped CDs), and 1.0 g/L N-doped CDs (SMY-glucose-1.0 N-doped CDs) was 99.6 ± 0.68, 99.4 ± 0.80, and 106.2 ± 5.56 mL/g VS, respectively. Similar to cellulose, there was no obvious difference on CH4 production between SMY-glucose-control and SMY-glucose-0.1 N-doped CDs. However, methane production of SMY-glucose-1.0 N-doped CDs was promoted, which was 6.63% higher than the SMY-glucose-control (p = 0.218), and for the maximum daily rate of CH4 has been significantly increased by 13.31% (p = 0.029) compared to SMY-glucose-control. Similarly, Zhao et al. (2015) found that graphite, biochar and carbon cloth all immediately promoted methane production and COD removal. In the experiment of glucose as substrate, the methanogenic efficiency of SMY-glucose-1.0 N-doped CDs was increased by 6.58%, while that of SMY-glucose-0.1 N-doped CDs was almost unchanged (Table S3).

Addition of N-doped CDs (1 g/L or 0.1 g/L) has shown different impacts on CH4 production of cellulose and glucose, indicating that addition of 0.1 g/L N-doped CDs was probably a way too low to affect AD performance or CH4 production. Further investigation should be carried on with narrowed testing concentration. Thus, the applied CDs concentration was altered from 0.1 and 1 g/L to 1 and 5 g/L for vinegar residue and ethanol.

For vinegar residue, the cumulative methane production of control (SMY-vinegar residue-control), vinegar residue with 1 g/L N-doped CDs (SMY-vinegar residue-1.0 N-doped CDs) and vinegar residue with 5 g/L N-doped CDs (SMY-vinegar residue-5.0 N-doped CDs) was determined to be 33.3 ± 4.4, 46.1 ± 5.2, and 109.9 ± 2.9 mL/g VS, respectively (Figs. 5e and f). Application of vinegar residue-5.0 N-doped CDs increased both the methane production and maximum methane production rate dramatically. The methane yield increased by 230% (p = 0.000) compared to SMY-vinegar residue-control while the highest daily methane production rate was 216% (p = 0.020) higher than SMY-vinegar residue-control. The cumulative methane production of SMY-vinegar residue-1.0 N-doped CDs was 46.1 ± 5.0 mL/g VS, which was an increase of 38.4% compared to the SMY-vinegar residue-control; the maximum methane production rate was 10.51 ± 3.5 mL/g VS/d, which was which was an increase of 52.3% compared to the SMY-vinegar residue-control. In addition, the methanogenic efficiency of SMY-vinegar residue-5.0 N-doped CDs increased by 229.64% and the methanogenic efficiency of SMY-vinegar residue-1.0 N-doped CDs increased by 38.25% (Table S3). The reason why 5.0 g/L N-doped CDs promotes methanogenesis better is maybe because the concentration of N-doped CDs in the system is larger, and it is easier to contact a large number of microorganisms and promote interspecies electron transfer. Consistent with this, Yan et al. found that GAC promoted microbial activity during AD with glucose as a raw material, and shortened start-up period (Yan et al., 2017).

At the end, AD of ethanol was conducted to compare the impact of N-doped CDs and Zn-doped CDs. As shown in Figs. 5g and h, the methane production of control (SMY-ethanol-control), N-doped CDs (1 and 5 g/L) – (SMY-ethanol-1.0 N-doped CDs and SMY-ethanol-5.0 N-doped CDs), Zn-doped CDs (1 and 5 g/L) – (SMY-ethanol-1.0 Zn-doped CDs and SMY-ethanol-5.0 Zn-doped CDs) is 422.8 ± 24.4; 432.2 ± 3.5; 408.5 ± 34.5; 456.6 ± 11.6, and 526.7 ± 2.8 m L/g VS, respectively. Different to other substrates (cellulose, glucose, or vinegar residue), application of N-doped CDs had very limited effect on promoting AD performance at low concentration (1 g/L), which turned to be negative by increasing from 1 to 5 g/L. N-doped CDs was probably toxic to microorganism responsible for converting ethanol to methane, which therefore inhibited the process with large amount of N-doped CDs existed in the system. In contrast, SMY-ethanol-5.0 Zn-doped CDs increased methane production by 24.59% (p = 0.396) compare to SMY-ethanol-control. Moreover, the maximum daily methane production rate with SMY-ethanol-5.0 Zn-doped CD increased 85.21% (p = 0.000) compared to SMY-ethanol-control. However, Lin et al. found the best promotion effect of graphene can increase methane production by 25% (Lin et al., 2017). AD of ethanol adding with 5 g/L Zn-doped CDs improved 24.59% of the methanogenic efficiency to a larger extent compare to SMY-ethanol-control (Table S3). Similarly, Zhao et al. (2016) prove that activated carbon (GAC) can improve the mutual metabolism of ethanol-enriched propionic acid/butyric acid, but has little effect on traditional enrichment. Therefore, GAC can increase DIET during AD of ethanol. Zn-doped CDs showed higher activity than N-doped CDs on the effect of methanogenesis during AD of ethanol. It was proven that carbonaceous materials such as graphite, biochar, and carbon cloth are capable of promoting methane fermentation and chemical oxygen demand removal (Zhao et al., 2015).

CDs can promote cumulative CH4 production in the AD of the above four substrates (cellulose, glucose, ethanol, vinegar residue). It is speculated that the reasons may be: (1) N-doped CDs particles are small, the specific surface area is large, and the water solubility is particularly good. Thus, it can be widely distributed in the liquid phase of AD. (2) The electrical conductivity of N-doped CDs (890 μs/cm, concentration: 1 g/L) and Zn-doped CDs (6.29 ms/cm, concentration: 1 g/L) is better than the fimbriae on the surface of microorganisms. It can be adsorbed on the surface of methanogenic microorganisms or between mutual microbial aggregates to replace the fimbriae on the surface of these microorganisms to carry out more effective electron transfer and accelerate AD. Similarly, Liu et al. found that GAC provides a surface for cells to attach, and establishes a short electron transfer distance between microorganisms, thereby promoting DIET (Liu et al., 2012). (3) The added CDs can enrich in part of the co-operating microorganisms (Fig. 7), such as Pseudomonadaceae, Methanosarcinaceae) or directly metabolizing methanogenic microorganisms (such as Methanobacteriaceae) that can participate in electron transfer, which can enhance the AD of feedstocks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the kinetic models for AD
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 6, the experimental value refers to the actual methane production during the experiment process, and the simulated value refers to the theoretical methane production simulated by the modified Gompertz model and cone model. The linear relationship between the simulation value (modified Gompertz models and cone models) and the experimental value of each run was presented in Fig. 6. The simulation worked well as the R2 is 0.9436 (Fig. 6), which is higher than the value (0.75) (Li et al., 2018).

The estimated parameters by modeling of different feedstocks with CDs addition are listed in
Tables 5 and 6. Both two models were well-fitted with the gas production with R2 ranged between 0.9163 and 0.9931 and 0.9190–0.9941, respectively. RMSE represents the standard error between the simulated and experimental values while the lower RMSE normally indicates better fitting. AICc is a method for choose model that performs the best among a set of candidate models. The second order (corrected) AICc can be positive or negative while lower AICc value indicates better fitting.

For vinegar residue, CDs addition could significantly improve the maximum CH4 production rate (μ
m ); SMY-glucose-1.0 N-doped CDs increased μ
m to 13.70 mL/g VS CH4 day–1, compared to SMY-glucose-control (12.15 mL/g VS CH4 day–1); SMY-vinegar residue-5.0 N-doped CDs increased μ
m to 16.29 mL/g VS CH4 day–1, which was four times to that of SMY-vinegar residue-control (3.98 mL CH4 day–1). In addition, SMY-ethanol-5.0 Zn-doped CDs decreased the lag phase (λ) from 1.68 to 0.63 days. As a fibrous feedstock, addition of CDs did not alter the lag phase of vinegar residue. Due to the difficulty of degradation of vinegar residue, the effect of CDs is not obvious for λ, however, the influence of CDs on cellulose is clearly changed from 13.29 ± 0.71 day–1 to 5.49 ± 1.00 day–1. The result suggests that with the addition of CDs, a lower λ and higher μm are observed. Here, higher λ indicates slower start-up, and lower μ
m indicates lower digestion efficiency. Consistently, in the digestion system with CDs, with the increasing of concentration, the daily methane rate and cumulative methane production increased. Consistently, Yan et al. (2017) carbon nanotubes can improve microbial activity and shorten the start-up period of the test. In conclusion, the addition of CDs not only increases cumulative methane production but also shortens the time to reach the maximum rate, which consistent with the previous observation.

In Table 6, for Cone models (Li et al., 2018), the R2 ranged from 0.9190 to 0.9941, indicating that methane production could well be explained by the model. Based on the results of the Cone model, k is improved by addition of CDs. The k value of control cellulose; 0.1 g/L N-doped CDs of cellulose and 1 g/L N-doped CDs of cellulose have been determined to be 0.054 ± 0.002; 0.060 ± 0.001; 0.080 ± 0.004 day–1, respectively. It is believed that higher k values can be obtained by adding more CDs. High k value means high hydrolysis rate, which is helpful for improving biodegradability and methane yield. Generally, with the concentration of CDs increasing, higher k values are found.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of CDs on microbial community
ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial community analysis is shown in Fig. 7. During AD process, bacteria convert macromolecular insoluble organics into fatty acids, hydrogen, acetate, and carbon dioxide. The main bacterial communities in all samples were Pseudomonadaceae (9.98−17.36%) and Heliobacteriaceae (2.49−11.62%). Similarly, a large number of Porphyromonadaceae (4.01–14.49%), Caldicoprobacteraceae (2.36−6.26%) and Ruminococcaceae (2.36−2.85%) were also found in the samples. The relative abundance of Pseudomonadaceae increased from 9.98% (control group) to 17.36% in the liquid digestate, but the content of Heliobacterium decreased, from 11.62% to 2.49% in the liquid digestate (Fig. 7b). Studies have pointed out that Pseudomonadaceae is an electricity-producing bacteria responsible for converting VFA into electric current in microbial fuel cells, which can also convert ethanol into acetic acid (Freguia et al., 2010). In addition, Heliobacteriaceae, which belong to the thick-walled phylum, could generate energy through photosynthesis. As shown in Fig. 7, the relative abundance of Heliobacteriaceae from the solid digestate after AD is higher than that in the liquid digestate, especially with CDs addition. The corresponding Heliobacteriaceae in the sample is transferred from the liquid digestate to solid digestate. Due to the large amount of vinegar residue in the solid digestate, it is speculated that the Heliobacteriaceae is probably a strain originally contained in the vinegar residue.

In addition, the relative abundance of Ruminococcaceae and Caldicoprobacteriaceae remained similar from 2.36% to 2.85% in the liquid digestate and was doubled (from 3.19% to 6.26%) in the solid digestate. The image of SEM also indicated CDs can enrich microorganisms to promote AD (Fig. S1). Caldicoprobacteraceae is a thermophilic protein hydrolysate, which appertains to the order Clostridiales, phylum Firmicutes, as well as Geobacter, a well-known exoelectrogen that be able to conduct DIET (Hu et al., 2017). Ruminococcaceae also belongs to phylum Firmicutes, which can consume cellulose, starch, and protein (Paul et al., 2010), plays an essential role in the degradation of plant cytoderm and pass electrons to the receptor through DIET. In addition, the abundance of Syntrophomonadaceae increased from 3.57% to 4.15% in the liquid digestate. Syntrophomonadaceae has been used to improve the stability of the AD system, which can carry out DIET (Luo et al., 2015; Shrestha et al., 2014). Meanwhile, it also confirmed to have DIET capability by assisting on converting ethanol into acetate and producing electrons during the process (Freguia et al., 2010). As we all know, during AD, lignin of vinegar residue is not easily degraded by microorganisms, and its methane yield is very low. This also explains the reason why the actual methane production of the control group of vinegar residue is low. After adding conductive CDs, it can aggregate cellulose-decomposing bacteria to a certain extent, such as Ruminococcaceae, and protein-decomposing bacteria, such as Caldicoprobacteraceae. The increased electron transfer rate and increased metabolic activity among microorganisms make the components that are not easily degradable, such as lignin, can be degraded to a large extent to produce methane. Therefore, the rate of methane production and the cumulative production are greatly improved (Fig. 4). In summary, the addition of CDs to an AD system can enrich microorganisms to improve potential capability of DIET, thereby improving the performance of AD and increasing methane production.

The relative abundance of archaea in the methanogenic process is demonstrated in the Figs. 7c and d. At order level, Methanosarcinales (>60%) were the majority in all samples, followed by Methanomicrobiales, as shown in Fig. 6c. As is known to us, Methanosarcinales is one of the known methanogenic archaea that can participate in DIET, and it can directly receive electrons to reduce CO2 to CH4 (Shrestha et al., 2014). Due to the excellent electrical conductivity of CDs, the rate of electron transfer between microorganisms and the efficiency of methanogenesis has been accelerated. Furthermore, the methane production was enhanced by the increase of Methanomicrobiales from 24% to 44% for solid digestate and from 8% to 16% for liquid digestate resulted by addition of CDs. Since Methanobacteriaceae is a kind of bacteria that specifically metabolizes organic matter, the increasing of this bacteria content is conducive to the AD of methanogenesis.

The impact of CDs addition on the relative abundance of microorganisms has been discussed. However, the biological interactions of other bacteria in the anaerobic digester (e.g., Clostridiales) is still unknown. Co-occurrence network ( Fig. 8) reflects that various microbes actively proliferate under various niches. As shown in Fig. 8, Clostridiales is the most abundant microorganism which has a wide niche，which can be used as a signal source to transmit signals to other species. Clostridiales and Methnobactriales were positively correlated, suggesting that they may share niches, which determine the composition and diversity of these species. In addition, Clostridiales has the metabolic capacity of a variety of organic molecules (Ju et al., 2017), including hydrolysis of cellulose and protein (Sebaihia et al., 2007; Xia et al., 2013), polysaccharide fermentation (Vanwonterghem et al., 2014), acetate-producing reaction and homoacetogenicization (Schink, 1984), simultaneous oxidation of fatty acids and acetic acid (Stieb and Schink, 1985). Therefore, niche differentiation can drive a versatile Clostridiales. The metabolic versatility and phenotypic plasticity using a wide range of organic microscopic and macromolecules should allow them to perform multiple functions in the anaerobic digester and occupy a wide niche. Therefore, the non-overlapping niche between Clostridiales and other bacterial populations (JG30-KF-CM45, Methnomicrobiales, Thermoanaerobacteriales) may enable their co-production in anaerobic digesters.

A major bacteria and archaea microbial network map involved in the AD of ethanol is demonstrated in Fig. 9. With no CDs addition, ethanol is first degraded by acidogenic bacteria, which includes Ruminococcaceae (2.36%) and Caldicoprobacteriaceae (3.19%). Intermediates, such as CH3COOH and H2 (or protons and electrons) are produced via acidification, following with methanogenesis of where methane is formed. The existence of conductive CDs in the AD process fundamentally changes the syntrophic interactions between the acidogenic bacteria and methanogenic archaea (Fig. 8b). Ruminococcaceae (2.85%) served as the dominant acidogenic bacteria, whereas Caldicoprobacteriaceae (6.26%) was enriched, which indicated Caldicoprobacteriaceae may play a vital role in ethanol degradation. As mentioned earlier, Caldicoprobacteriaceae are capable of carrying out DIET. Archaea Methanomicrobiaceae (23.56%) and Methanosarcinaceae (63.10%) are capable of reducing carbon dioxide using free electrons. Therefore, enrichment of these microorganisms in methanogenesis proposes a possibility to act as a crucial role in conducting DIET.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparing electron flux between DIET and IHT
ce:section.ce:para: Propionate, as one of the major intermediates in the AD process, is difficult to disintegrate into acetate due to its thermodynamically unfavorable. Therefore, it is likely to be accumulated in AD system which can be used to determine the rate of DIET and IHT (see the Supporting Information for specific calculation methods). Omh’s law is used to measure the maximum electron carrier flux (Cruz Viggi et al., 2014). For the N-doped CDs, the electrical conductivity (σ) was 890 µs/cm, and the cross-sectional area of the electron conduit was assumed to be 7.85 × 103 nm 2. The maximum redox potential (ΔE) (4Propionate– + 3H2O = 4Acetate– + HCO3 – + H+ + 3CH4) was about 0.067 V and the maximum electron carrier flux for DIET of methanogenesis was 9.36 × 10–11 A. For Zn-doped CDs, the maximum electron flux was 6.62 × 10–10 mol·s–1, which shown that using Zn-doped CDs is superior to N-doped CDs. Moreover, the conductivity of N-doped CDs (1.0 g/L) and Zn-doped CDs (1.0 g/L) was 890 μS·cm–1 and 6.29 mS·cm–1, respectively, which led to the different impacts on methane production. The rate of electron transfer between the microbes in the methane production process was also higher with Zn-doped CDs addition, which increased the efficiency of methanogenesis.

IHT can be measured by Fick's law of mass transfer. The maximum ([H2]Ace) and minimum ([H2]Met) hydrogen concentration by the acetogen (ΔG’ = 0) was about 74.80 × 10–9 mol·L-1 and 12.78 × 10–9 mol·L-1, respectively. The diffusion coefficient of H2 (Df) in water at 25 °C was 4.5 × 10–5 cm2·s-1. The microbial surface area was about 3.14 m2. Thus, the maximum H2 flux was about 1.75 × 10–18 mol/s, the electric current related to the electron carrier flux for IHT of bicarbonate reduction was about 3.38 × 10–13 A. It is concluded that the electronic flux of DIET is about 103 times that of IHT. Therefore, DIET of methanogenesis has a higher kinetic than IHT of bicarbonate reduction after adding CDs.

This study clearly revealed positive effects of adding CDs on methanogenesis of AD. Addition of 5 g/L Zn-doped CDs promoted AD efficiency by maximum 85.21% (p = 0.000) with 526.7 ± 2.8 mL/g VS of CH4 production acquired. Microbiological analysis showed that the abundance of exoelectrogens increased greatly, which indicated that addition of CDs accelerated the DIET. Meanwhile, correlation-based network analysis of species co-occurrence and co-exclusion patterns revealed Clostridiales species may be used as a signal source to transmit signals to other species. Electron flux calculation showed that adding CDs promoted mainly on DIET rather than IHT. These findings could strengthen understanding on the utilization of CDs as an effective conductive media for microbes and improve AD performance.
